title: Climate Policy Reversals & The Rise of Placeholder Relationships
content:
The Environmental Protection Agency’s recent repeal of its endangerment finding has been framed by the current administration as a massive victory for American industry. However, this ideological project is widely expected to result in significant marketplace confusion and endless legal battles. Because this finding underpins all noteworthy climate rules, its removal leaves a massive regulatory void that individual states will attempt to fill. Meanwhile, major corporations will be forced to independently decide to what degree they should continue pursuing climate-friendly initiatives.

The original endangerment finding was established after the Supreme Court ruled that the EPA had a legal obligation to regulate greenhouse gases if they harmed public health. Following a comprehensive study in 2009, the EPA concluded that climate change indeed posed a severe threat, leading to a slew of progressive emissions regulations. These rules, ranging from tailpipe standards to power-plant restrictions, played a crucial role in advancing U.S. climate progress. Although economic shifts and declining costs have also naturally stimulated clean energy, strong regulations provided essential structural guidance for the market.

Looking forward, global companies will continue facing intense regulatory pressure in Europe, Asia, and Latin America, regardless of U.S. policy reversals. They also recognize that a future administration could easily restore the endangerment finding and reinstate all its associated regulations. In completely rupturing existing climate efforts, the current government has created an unpredictable blank slate. While no one knows exactly what the new regulatory framework will look like, experts agree that building a more resilient system from this fracture is imperative.
